texts.orig: Lithium is widely applied in many fields such as ceramics and glass, batteries, lubricating greases, continuous casting mold flux powders, air treatment, polymer production, and primary aluminium production [1]. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Salt lake brine is an abundant lithium resource and has great developing value and potentiality. But the Mg/Li ratio of some salt lakes in China and Dead Sea in Jordan is extremely high. In this study, electrodialysis (ED) with monovalent selective ion-exchange membranes was used to investigate the separation performance of Li + /Mg 2+ from synthetic multinary mixture. The effects of operating conditions on the lithium recovery, permselectivity, and Mg/Li ratio of the product were evaluated. The results indicate that ED with monovalent selective ion-exchange membranes has a significant separation effect on Li + /Mg 2+ . When the optimized parameters were used in ED, the Mg/Li mass ratio of the product stream was reduced to 8.0 (18.8 times compared to a feed Mg/Li ratio of 150), and simultaneously, the Li + recovery reached to 95.3%. Compared to nanofiltration, EDexhibited superiority both technically and economically for the fractionation of Li + /Mg 2+ . It was also verified that the presence of co-existing cations would not be detrimental to the feasibility of lithium extraction using ED. Meanwhile, the separation mechanism of ED process was discussed qualitatively.

Western China is abundant in saline lithium resource, however the Mg/Li ratio of brine is extremely high, and the grade of Li + is extremely low as compared to that of Na + and Mg 2+ ions [5]. The conventional precipitation -crystallization technique is not suitable for such type of saline resource. The adsorption method has been studied as an economic and environmentally friendly technology from high Mg/Li ratio brines [5 -10]. There are also developing membrane separation processes of Li + / Mg 2+ . Nanofiltration (NF) has been reported for the ion fractionation of Li + and Mg 2+ in the literatures [11 -14]. Wen et al. [11] reported that the selectivity of Li + /Mg 2+ is relatively high; however, the lithium recovery is limited, and the membrane is not cost-effective as compared to electrodialysis (ED). Furthermore, NF was applied to deal with the brine with a relatively low Mg/Li ratio (20 -60) as recently reported [11 -14].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Conventional ED and related processes have been widely applied in the study of brackish water or seawater desalination [15 -19], industrial wastewater treatment [20 -23] and food industry [24 -27]. The conventional ion exchange membrane only has the selectivity between their counter-ions and co-ions. The selective ion-selective exchange membrane has considerable differences in the transport number between ions with the same charge but different valences. Based on the monovalent selective ion-exchange membrane (IEM) material's characteristic of the strong retardation of divalent ions [28,29] and high permeability of monovalent ions, the fractionation of monovalent ions from divalent ions is certainly possible by ED with monovalent selective ion-exchange membranes. Kabay [28,30] and Kim [31] studied the selective separation of monovalent and divalent ions, nevertheless, the concentrations of monovalent ions and divalent ions are relatively close to each other. The feed compositions in these studies were not consistent with real seawater or brine. In this study, the feed composition varied with relatively wide range of the Mg/Li ratio, and the concentration difference between Li + and Mg 2+ in feed solution was significant. In addition, multi-component aqueous system was used as feed to investigate the effect of co-existing ions on Li + /Mg 2+ separation. These characteristics of feed solution are representative of typical natural salt lake brines. The separation performance of Li + /Mg 2+ using ED has not been comprehensively studied before. This study is focused on the comprehensive investigation of Li + /Mg 2+ separation performance with a high Mg/Li mass ratio feed, aiming at significantly reducing the Mg/Li ratio by ED. Therefore, a new direction for developing salt lake lithium resources was raised. Meanwhile, the separation mechanism of the process was discussed qualitatively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The performance of ED process not only depends on the film forming characteristics of membrane material, but also depends on the operating conditions such as temperature, flow condition, osmotic pressure, and current density etc. Therefore, the effects of operating parameters including temperature, flow rate, the initial volume of the product stream, and current density on the separation performance were investigated then. The feed solution contained three different ions: lithium, magnesium, and chloride. The initial concentrations of Li + and Mg 2+ ions were 0.15 and 22.5 g/L, respectively, thus, the Mg/Li mass ratio of the feed solution was 150 during all experiments. The initial volume of the feed solution in the dilute compartment was 25 L in all experiments. The temperature of the solution was controlled by a heat exchanger. Different temperatures (15 -30 °C) were used in the experiments to investigate the temperature effect on the separation performance. Different flow rates of the feed stream (0.6 -1.2 m 3 /h, i.e., linear velocities of 4.3 -8.5 m/s, accordingly) were used in the experiments to determine the effect of the flow rate on the separation performance, and the flow rate was kept constant during each batch. Different initial volumes of the product stream (15 -25 L, i.e., V C/ V D values of 0.6 -1.0, accordingly) were used to determine the effect of the volumes of the product stream on the separation performance. Different current intensities (0.3 -0.7 A, i.e., current densities of 5.9 -13.8 A/m 2 , accordingly) were used in the experiments to investigate the effect of the current density on the separation performance.

texts.orig: 2.3. Investigation of co-existing cations permeation
texts.text: 2.3. Investigation of co-existing cations permeation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The feed composition may determine the separation performance of ED process. As shown in Table 2, in salt lake brine, the concentrations of co-existing cations such as Na + , K + , and Ca 2+ cannot be negligible as compared to Li + . To determine the priority of various cationic species for competitive permeation in lithium-containing aqueous system, as well as the cations-partitioning mechanism, the permeation experiment of the various ionic species was carried out. LiCl, NaCl, KCl, MgCl2, and CaCl2 were used to prepare the feed solution -a Li + , Na + , K + , Mg 2+ , Ca 2+ /Cl --H2O multinary electrolyte solution system. The concentration of each cation in feed solution was 0.05 mol/L. The experiment was operated under a current density of 13.8 A/m 2 with a feed linear velocity of 7.1 cm/s. The temperature of the solution was controlled at 25 °C.

texts.orig: The curves of the recovery rate of Li + and leak rate of Mg 2+ at different feed Mg/Li ratios are shown in Fig. 2. After 3.0 h ED under a constant current mode (0.3 A), with different feed Mg/Li ratios, the recovery rate of Li + was as high as 100%. The leak rate of Mg 2+ was limited to b 12%, indicating that a major portion of Mg 2+ ions is retained in dilute stream. A high recovery of lithium can be achieved by this method when feed Mg/Li mass ratio is lower than 300, and a satisfactory rejection of magnesium can be achieved by this method during the full range of feed Mg/ Li ratio covered. All the curves of the leak rate of Mg 2+ are linear during 3.0 h operating period, indicating that the permeating speed of Mg 2+ is almost constant. This observation also indicates that the concentration gradient of Mg 2+ on the membrane surface slightly changes, for the migration rate of divalent ions is extremely low.

texts.orig: It also can be observed from Fig. 2 that both the recovery rate of Li + and leak rate of Mg 2+ decreased with increasing Mg/Li ratio of feed brine, this phenomenon may owe to the formation of electric double layer (EDL) at the ion-exchange membrane -solution interface (Fig. 3). Moya [33] numerically studied the theory of the formation of EDL at the ion-exchange membrane -solution interface, suggesting that during the process of formation of the EDL at the interface, the electric charge density in the diffusion boundary layer is positive and it arises from the accumulation of cations at the interfacial region. On the basis of the theory mentioned above, the separation mechanism of this process can be derived from the interpretation of EDL at the ion-exchange membrane -solution interface. The cation-exchange membrane (CEM) is consisted of porosities and fixed charged groups (sulfonic acid radicals). As shown in Fig. 3, divalent counter-ions were preferentially adsorbed on the fixed charged groups in CEM, and thus thought to be migrated across the CEM slowly [28,29]. In this process, Mg 2+ ions are accumulated at the interfacial region. A positive electric layer is formed by the accumulation of Mg 2+ ions at the membrane -solution interface. Thus, the counter-ions at the diffusion boundary layer adjacent to CEM are excluded by this positive electric layer. The local electrostatic repulsive force acting on Li + ions is weaker than that acting on Mg 2+ ions, for the positive charge density of Li + ion is lower than Mg 2+ ion's. Hence, Mg 2+ ions penetrate the positive charge layer harder, compared to Li + ions, resulting that Li + ions permeate ahead of Mg 2+ ions. When Mg/Li ratio increased, more Mg 2+ ions were accumulated at the interfacial region, thereby increasing the positive electric charge density at the diffusion boundary layer, and also causing an enhanced steric hindrance effect [34]. With respect to a certain counter-ion, the interaction of EDL

texts.orig: The curves of the Mg/Li mass ratio of the concentrate stream with different feed Mg/Li ratios ranging from 66.7 to 400 are shown in Fig. 4. After 3.0 h operating time, the Mg/Li mass ratio of each batch decreased remarkably (in the range of 6.7 -21.8 times) as compared to their initial feed concentrations, fulfilling the demand for the followup sodium carbonate precipitation process to recover lithium. It also can be seen that the Mg/Li ratio of concentrate stream increased with higher operating time, which was due to the fact that Li + ions were depleted in the dilute stream, thus, more Mg 2+ ions was required to permeate the CEM to maintain the electrical neutrality in the solution. When the feed Mg/Li mass ratio was b 150, the Mg/Li mass ratio of the product solution decreased to b 10. These observations indicate that the ion fractionation of Li + /Mg 2+ was effectively achieved by ED using Selemion CSO cation-exchange membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The curves of the recovery rate of Li + and leak rate of Mg 2+ under different temperatures ranging from 15 to 30 °C are shown in Fig. 5. The recovery rate of Li + reached to a plateau at 15 °C (95.6%) after 3.0 h operating time, whereas this value dropped to 73.5% for the experimentoperated at 30 °C. The viscosity of solution with higher concentration is decreased with the increasing of temperature, strengthening the mass transfer by diffusion. Whereas the conductivity of feed solution is increased with increasing temperature, resulting in a decline of potential gradient, which is the driving force of the mass transfer by electromigration. Thereby, the permeation of Li + ions was strengthened at lower temperature. Fig. 5 indicates that the recovery rate of Li + decreased with increasing temperature, whose variation is in accordance with the interpretation explained above. In view of this observation, it is suggested that the ions permeation performance in ED is primarily determined by the conductivity of solution, compared to the viscosity of solution. In this process, due to the high permselectivity of CEM and high concentration of Mg 2+ ions in feed solution, the conductivity of dilute stream can be maintained at a high value, which is in favour of the permeation of Li + ions.

texts.orig: The experiments at various current densities from 5.9 to 13.8 A/m 2 were compared. Transferred electric charge ( I · t ) is shown on the Xaxis to compare the separation performance of Li + /Mg 2+ under different current densities. The curves of the recovery rate of Li + and leak rate of Mg 2+ versus electric charge are shown in Fig. 8, indicating that the migration rates of both Li + and Mg 2+ decreased with increasing current density. Moreover, the leak rate of Mg 2+ showed a more remarkable downtrend with increasing current density as compared to the recovery rate of Li + , increasing S Li/Mg and decreasing Mg/Li mass ratio of the product stream as shown in Fig. 9. At higher current density, more charge was carried by Li + ions and less Mg 2+ was migrated, which resulted in a lower Mg/Li ratio in concentrate stream (a higher residual Mg/Li ratio in the dilute can be found simultaneously). This observation is consistent with the conclusion obtained by Galama [35]. The diagram also indicates that the plateaus of S Li/Mg are observed for the experiments at current densities of 9.9 and 13.8 A/m 2 . The highest S Li/Mg is 33.0 and 32.1 for the experiments at 9.9 and 13.8 A/m 2 , respectively. For the experiment at 9.9 and 13.8 A/m 2 , the Mg/Li ratio of the product stream increased from 5.1 to 7.2 and 4.9 to 6.3, respectively, lower than the experiments operated at 5.9 A/m 2 . Since the primary aim of this work is to substantially reduce the Mg/Li ratio, higher current density within a certain limit is beneficial to the fractionation of Li + /Mg 2+ , regardless of the economic aspects.

The experiments performed at lower current densities (5.9 and 9.9 A/m 2 ) hadhigher current efficiencies, probably due to the enhanced associated effects in ED. When higher current density is applied, there are not enough Li + ions to replenish the transported Li + from the diffusion boundary layer to the membrane surface. As a result, a few of the secondary effects in ED, concentration polarization and water splitting would be enhanced and contribute more to the current with increasing current density, thereby decreasing the effective electric current acting on the transport of target ions. Considering the separation effect and economic aspect comprehensively, it is suggested that 9.9 A/m 2 is an optimal value of current density for the ion-fractionation of Li + /Mg 2+ .

texts.orig: Table 3 shows a comprehensive comparison of the separation performance on Li + /Mg 2+ and economical characteristic between NF and ED. It is obvious that ED exhibited a higher lithium recovery, a higher permselectivity and a lower specific energy consumption compared to NF [11,12]. In this study, the permselectivity index S Li/Mg reached relatively high values ranging of 20.2 -33.0 with a high Li + recovery rate of 94.5%, and simultaneously, the specific energy consumption index P was only 0.0019 kWh/g Li + . The difference of P between ED and NF is orders of magnitude high. Based on the separation performance and energy consumption comprehensively, ED exhibited superiority to NF both technically and economically for the fractionation of Li + /Mg 2+ .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3.2. Effect of co-existing cations
texts.text: 3.3.2. Effect of co-existing cations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Section 3.3.1, it was derived that monovalent cations such as Na + and K + would generate remarkable competitive permeation along with Li + . The curves of the recovery rate of Li + and leak rate of Mg 2+ with different cationic species adding are shown in Fig. 12. It can be observed from the diagrams that the permeation of Li + as well as Mg 2+ was negatively affected by the adding of cations, among which K + significantly affected Li + recovery. Apparently, the effect of co-existing cations is in terms of the permeation sequence of cations, which is determined by steric hindrance as suggested in Section 3.3.1. For the experiments with the adding of Na + or Ca 2+ , the Li + recovery can reach over 80% yet, simultaneously the Mg 2+ leakage was lower than the blank experiment's, which is due to the competitive permeation of cations. Table 5 exhibits the Mg/Li mass ratio of product stream with different cations adding, indicating that the presence of Na + or Ca 2+ benefits reducing the Mg/Li ratio, whereas the presence of K + has no effect on it due to the remarkable competitive permeation with Li + . In general, the brine for lithium recovery is after potassium recovering process, thus, the presence of K + would not be a notable problem, though the presence of K + is detrimental to Li + recovery.

texts.orig: ( b 10) with a high recovery rate of Li + ( N 90%) for the mixture with a Mg/Li mass ratio of 150, which is suitable for the subsequent precipitation process for the extraction of lithium. Then, the effect of various conditions on the separation performance was investigated, indicating that appropriately lower temperature, higher flow rate, and higher current density [35] are in favour of the ion-fractionation of Li + and Mg 2+ . The volume ratio ' V C/ V D ' determines the separation performance remarkably in the batch mode ED with an optimized range of 0.8 to 1.0. The results also indicate that the ED stack has a higher current efficiency at a lower current intensity. Compared to NF [11,12], ED with ion-selective membrane exhibits a more effective separation performance and is more cost-effective too. The effect of co-existing cations on Li + /Mg 2+ separation was also determined. The result indicates that ED is feasible for the Li + recovery from multinary electrolyte solution system. The mass transfer and separation mechanism of the process were also discussed qualitatively. In ED process, the conductivity of solution plays a significant role for the mass transfer as compared to other physical properties. The permselectivity of this process was governed by the combination of electric double layer as well as steric hindrance.
